IA No. 6750/2025 By plaintiff u/O I Rule 10 CPC seeking impleadment of newly identified associated domains/URLs/UIs, their domain name registrar engaged in infringing activities as defendants (associated rogue domains/URLs/UIs) & extension of ad-interim order dated 10.02.2025) The said IA is taken up on request of ld. counsel for the plaintiff. The plaintiff is seeking to implead additional rogue websites & their domain name registrars as the defendant nos. 81 to 96 since This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 16:32:07 they are illegally distributing the copyrighted content of the plaintiff. The relevant details of these infringers are mentioned in para no.6 of the application. Keeping in view, the contents of this application so read in the light of the prayers made in the suit, the same is allowed. It is worthwhile to note that the additional rogue websites & their domain name registrars impleaded as the defendant nos. 81 to 96 shall be subject to the interim orders already passed in the suit. The amended memo of parties be taken on record. Summons be issued to the defendant nos. 81 to 96 through all permissible modes, subject to the filing of PF. At this stage, ld. counsel for the plaintiff requests to issue summons through email only as the newly identified defendants are rogue websites, therefore the summons be issued via emails, subject to the filing of PF. IA No. 16678/2025 (By defendant no. 3 under proviso to Order V Rule 1 & Order VIII Rule 1 CPC r/w Section 151 CPC for condonation of delay in filing the written statement) As per service-affidavit of the plaintiff filed vide diary no. 2532558/2025, defendant no. 1 to 18 & 20 were served through emails on 17.03.2025. Ld. counsel for the plaintiff submits that there is a delay in filing the written statement by defendant no. 3, therefore the same may be condoned, subject to the heavy costs. The delay in filing the written statement by defendant no. 3 is condoned, subject to the costs of Rs. 10,000/- to be payable to other party. The defendant no. 3 is directed to take steps to bring the written statement on record and registry is also requested to do the needful. The costs be paid within a week. The time to file the replication shall only reckon after the payment of costs. timeline.detail: I.A. 14129/2025 (for Stay) 1. The plaintiff filed the captioned suit for permanent injunction restraining the illegal and unlawful activities by defendant nos.1 to 4 and other unkn…
timeline.orderText: $~43 * IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI + CS(COMM) 108/2025 STAR INDIA PVT LTD .....Plaintiff Through: Mr. Sidharth Chopra, Mr. Yatinder Garg, Ms. Disha Sharma and Ms. Rimjhim Tiwari and Ms. Isha Singh, Advs. versus IPTV SMARTER PRO & ORS. .....Defendants Through: Mr. Avish Sharma, Adv. for D-1, 4 & 36. Ms. Mrinal Ojha, Mr. Debarshi Dutta, Mr. Arjun Mookerjee, Ms. Nikita Rathi and Mr. Nikhil Gupta, Advs. for D-7. CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SAURABH BANERJEE O R D E R % 29.05.2025 I.A. 14129/2025 (for Stay) 1. The plaintiff filed the captioned suit for permanent injunction restraining the illegal and unlawful activities by defendant nos.1 to 4 and other unknown defendants (rogue websites) from infringement of copyright and broadcast reproduction rights. 2. In an accompanying application, vide order dated 10.02.2025 this Court had granted ex parte ad interim injunction to the plaintiff, whereby the said rogue websites as also their respective Domain Name Registrars (DNRs) were directed to lock and suspend all associated domains/ URLs/ UIs, as also to the Internet Service Providers (ISPs) to suspend access to This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:57:00 the same. Additionally, the plaintiff was also granted the liberty to implead newly identified domains/ URLs/ UIs, as and when discovered during the course of the proceedings. 3. Pursuant thereto, the plaintiff has been filing as many as seven impleadment applications for bringing on record three new mobile application (rogue mobile applications) and 16 new rogue domains/ URLs/ UIs engaged in illegally broadcasting the plaintiff’s content without authorisation. 4. Vide the present application, the plaintiff seeks further directions to facilitate timely and effective action against newly impleaded rogue websites as well as rogue mobile applications and their associated domains/ URLs/ UIs that are infringing the plaintiff’s exclusive content, and to enable real-time blocking of such platforms in relation to the ongoing and the upcoming sporting events, such as the Indian Premier League 2025, as also the forthcoming England Tour of India 2025 scheduled to commence during the vacation from 01.06.2025 to 30.06.2025, amongst others. 5. In support of the above, learned counsel appearing for the plaintiff submits that the procedural limitation of approaching this Court repeatedly on discovery of new websites and rogue mobile applications, especially in view of the upcoming vacation from 01.06.2025 to 30.06.2025, poses a serious risk of substantial and irreparable harm to the plaintiff especially in view of the imminent and high-value sporting events as aforementioned. Learned counsel hence prays that a case for grant of real- time blocking relief against rogue websites and rogue mobile applications that may be discovered has been made out by the plaintiff. This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:57:00 6. For the aforesaid, learned counsel for the plaintiff places reliance on the order passed by this Court in a separate suit bearing no.CS(COMM) 266/2025 entitled Star India Private Limited v. https//crichdplayer.org & Ors. wherein a real-time injunction was granted qua the Indian Premier League 2025, albeit with respect to rogue websites and not rogue mobile applications extending to their associated domains/ URLs/ UIs. 7. Learned counsel further places reliance on the case bearing no.CS (COMM) 688/2023 entitled Star India Pvt. Ltd. & Anr. v. Jiolive.Tv and Ors. wherein this Court emphasised the necessity of blocking rogue websites on a real-time basis in connection with sporting events, amongst numerous other orders passed by this Court wherein real-time relief has been granted. 8. In view thereof, learned counsel lastly submits that the prayers sought in the present application may be restricted only till 03.07.2025 and not any further. 9. Learned counsel for the defendant no.7 opposes the grant of the aforesaid relief(s), particularly, since the relief(s) sought for by the plaintiff in the present application are beyond the reliefs sought for in the present suit. 10. This Court has heard learned counsel for the parties as also gone through the pleadings and documents on record. 11. The plaintiff is the true owner of the exclusive rights in respect of its content, either originally made or through broadcasting rights acquired by it, and has also obtained an ex parte ad interim injunction order dated 10.02.2025 from this Court. There is also no doubt that the rogue websites, as also the rogue mobile application and their associated This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:57:00 domains/ URLs/ UIs are operating without any bona fide, with a predominant intention of infringing content on their interfaces and an organised nature of infringement, with complete disregard for the intellectual property rights of the plaintiff. 12. In the new age of technology, today it has become increasingly easy and convenient for infringers to create alpha-numeric/ mirror/ redirect variants of infringing websites, and by the time impleadment and extension of relief(s) can take place, certain time-sensitive infringing activities like live streaming of sporting events have already commenced illegally and by the time the effected party like the plaintiff approaches this Court, it is too late. Even otherwise, the post blocking scenario is also a race against time since new alpha-numeric/ mirror/ redirect variants of infringing websites spawn

timeline.orderText: $~21 * IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI + CS(COMM) 108/2025 STAR INDIA PVT LTD .....Plaintiff Through: Mr. Yatinder Garg, Ms. Rimjhim Tiwari, Ms. Ishi Singh & Ms. Disha Sharma, Advs. versus IPTV SMARTER PRO & ORS. .....Defendant Through: Mr. Mrinal Ojha, Mr. Debarshi Dutta, Mr. Yogesh Singh & Ms. Nikita Rathi, Advs. for D-7. CORAM: SIDHARTH MATHUR (DHJS), JOINT REGISTRAR (JUDICIAL) O R D E R % 01.05.2025 In view of its impleadment ordered today, issue summons to the defendant no. 36. This defendant has accepted the summons through counsel today in the court and the plaintiff shall now supply the copy of the plaint and the paper-book to its counsel, via email whereafter the written statement may be filed in accordance with law. In continuation of today’s impleadment, issue summons to the defendant no. 39 through all permissible modes, subject to the filing of PF. List the matter for the completion of service/pleadings on the date already fixed i.e. 01.07.2025. This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:57:00 IA No. 10861/2025 (By plaintiff u/s 151 CPC seeking an early hearing in the IA No. 10055/2025 in CS(COMM) 108/2025) Issue notice. Accepted. The counsel for the proposed defendant no. 36 states that an early hearing may be permitted. Accordingly this application is allowed, whereby an early hearing of the IA No. 10055/2025 is being conducted today itself. IA No. 10055/2025 (By plaintiff u/o 1 Rule 10 CPC seeking impleadment of newly identified application & Its associated domains engaged in infringing activities as original defendants (associated rogue domains/URLs/UIs) & extension of ad-interim order dated 10.02.2025) The plaintiff is seeking to implead the newly identified IPTV App i.e. Televizo as well as the impugned URL i.e. hybridtv.live on the ground that the copyrighted content of the plaintiff is being televised/distributed through them. The counsel for the Televizo App states that they are not involved in any distribution of the copyrighted content of the plaintiff, because they are merely a client side video player like VLC whereupon anyone and everyone can play their content without the active approval/knowledge/consent/permission of its developer/owner. He thus states that the impugned URL of hybridtv.live is being operated from this App without the active approval/knowledge/consent/involvement of anyone associated with Televizo. The counsel for the plaintiff states that the impugned broadcast, via hybridtv.live needs to be stopped and for that appropriate directions qua the concerned ISP & DNR must be issued This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:57:00 so as to prevent the illegal distribution of the copyrighted content of the plaintiff. In view of the aforesaid submissions and counter-submissions, I am of the view that the App named Televizo & the impugned URL i.e. hybridtv.live, may be impleaded as separate defendants instead of a single entity as prayed in the application, so that no adverse effect is caused to Televizo App or its operation wherein the distribution of the copyrighted content of the plaintiff is not involved. Accordingly the “Televizo” App IPTV player is impleaded as defendant no. 36, while the impugned URL “hybridtv.live” is separately impleaded as defendant no. 39. The application is accordingly disposed off. It is worthwhile to note that the additional mirror/re-direct/alphanumeric variation/URL of defendant no. 39 i.e. “hybridtv.live” shall be subject to the interim orders already passed in the suit and thus the concerned ISP & DNR shall do the needful for the implementation of those interim orders qua the defendant no. 39. It is made clear that this order has no bearing qua defendant no. 36 as far as the removal of the impugned URL of “hybridtv.live” is concerned. The amended memo of parties be now filed in due course. JUSTICE SAURABH BANERJEE O R D E R % 09.04.2025 1. Vide the present application under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, read with Section 151 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (CPC), the plaintiff seeks exemption from pre-litigation mediation. I.A. 9307/2025-Exp from pre institution mediation 2. Considering the averments made in the present application, as also since the plaintiff is seeking ex parte ad interim injunction, and in view of the order passed by the Hon’ble Supreme Court in Yamini Manohar v. T.K.D. Krithi 2024 (5) SCC 815, which has been followed by a Division Bench this Court in Chandra Kishore Chaurasia v. R. A. Perfumery Works Private Limited 2022:DHC:4454-DB, the plaintiff is exempted from instituting pre-litigation mediation. 3. Accordingly, the present application stands disposed of. This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:57:14 CS(COMM) 331/2025 Page 2 of 12 4. Exemption allowed, subject to all just exceptions. I.A. 9308/2025-Exp 5. The application stands disposed of. 6. Vide the present application under Section 80, read with Section 151 of the CPC, the plaintiff is seeking exemption from the requirement of advance service upon the defendant nos. 5, 8, 14 and 15, i.e., Bharat Sanchar Nigam Ltd. (BSNL), Mahanagar Telephone Nigam Ltd. (MTNL), the Department of Telecommunications (DoT), and the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MEITY) respectively, on the ground that no formal remedy/ relief as prescribed under the Copyright Act, 1957 (the Act), is being claimed against them and since urgent reliefs are also sought against other defendants in other accompanying applications, as also since they are only being arrayed to ensure compliance with any orders that may be passed by this Court. I.A. 9309/2025-Exempt the plaintiff from issuing notice to D-5, 8, 14 & 15 7. For the reasons stated in the present application, as also taking into account the aforesaid connected factors involv ed, it would be in the interest of justice if the plaintiff is granted exemption from effecting advance service upon the aforesaid defendant nos. 5, 8, 14 and 15, i.e., BSNL, MTNL, DoT, and MEITY respectively. 8. Accordingly, the present application stands disposed of. 9. The Ongoing Event commenced on 15.01.2026 and is scheduled to conclude on 06.02.2026, while the Upcoming Event is scheduled to be held between 07.02.2026 and 08.03.2026. The matches of the Ongoing Event are being played in Zimbabwe and Namibia, while the matches of the Upcoming Event will be played across major venues in India and Sri Lanka. 21. This is an application filed by the plaintiff seeking exemption from instituting pre-institution Mediation under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 (“CC Act”). 2. Sawlani, Adv. for D-6 Ms. Amee Rana, Ms. Sana Banyal and Mr. Debditya Saha, Advs. for D- 86 CORAM: HON'BLE MS. JUSTICE MANMEET PRITAM SINGH ARORA O R D E R % 15.09.2025 I.A. 22885/2025 (seeking impleadment of the necessary defendants) 1.This is an application filed by the plaintiff under Order I Rule 10 Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (‘CPC’) seeking impleadment of defendant nos. 21 to 86. 2.At the outset, learned counsel for the applicant/plaintiff states that, though the averments in the application pertaining to the proposed defendant Nos. 21 to 86 are correct, a typographical error has inadvertently crept into prayer clauses (b), (c), and (d). 2.1He states that on his oral request, the prayer clauses be corrected to read as under: This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:58:19 CS(COMM) 756/2025 Page 2 of 12 “... b.Implead the Domain name Registrars of the additional associated domain URLs/ UIs, i.e., Defendant Nos. 67 – 84, to the instant suit; c.Implead Binance Holdings Limited as Defendant No. 85; d.Implead Meta Platforms Inc. as Defendant No. 86; ” 3.It is noticed that the error is in reference to the defendant number. The same is corrected and has been highlighted in bold. The oral prayer of the plaintiff is accepted and the prayer shall read as above. 4.Learned counsel for the plaintiff submits that the proposed defendant nos. 21 to 66 are additional associated domain/URLs/UIs; proposed defendant nos. 67 to 84 are Domain Name Registrants (‘DNRs’) of the said additional domains; proposed defendant no. 85 is Binance Holding limited; and proposed defendant no. 86 is Meta Platforms Inc. 5.In view of the averments made in the application, the Court is satisfied that proposed defendant nos. 21 to 66 are necessary parties and defendant nos. 67 to 85 are proper parties for adjudication of the reliefs sought in the present suit; so as to stop the continuing infringing activities by the defendants. Accordingly, the application is allowed and defendant nos. 21 to 86 are hereby impleaded. 6.Amended memo of parties, filed along with this application, be taken on record. 7.Learned counsel for the plaintiff states that he will move an appropriate application for amending the plaint for incorporating necessary averments qua defendant nos. 21 to 86 within a period of one (1) week. This statement is taken on record. This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:58:19 CS(COMM) 756/2025 Page 3 of 12 8.With the aforesaid directions, the application is disposed of. I.A. 22889/2025 (under Order XXXIX Rules 1 & 2 CPC) 9.This is an application filed by the plaintiff under Order XXXIX Rules 1 and 2 CPC seeking injunction against defendant nos. 21 to 66 from infringing copyright and broadcast reproduction rights of the Plaintiff by unlawfully providing access to the Plaintiff's exclusive content, including live channels and films, without authorization, and seeking directions to defendant nos. 67 to 86 as well as defendant nos. 6, 8 to 18. 10.Learned counsel for the applicant/plaintiff, at the outset, states that typographical errors have crept in the prayer clauses of the captioned application. 10.1He states that on his oral request, the prayer clauses be corrected. He states that the defendant number has been mentioned incorrectly at prayer clause (ii), (iii), (vii) and (viii) to read as under: “ i.Pass an order of ex-parte injunction restraining the Defendant Nos. 21 to 66, their owners, partners, proprietors, officers, servants, affiliates, employees, and all others in capacity of principal or agent acting for and on its behalf, or anyone claiming through, by or under it, from in any manner communicating to the public, hosting, storing, reproducing, streaming, broadcasting, re-broadcasting, causing to be seen or heard by public on payment of charge and/or making available for viewing the Plaintiff’s Exclusive Content through any app / domains / Rogue Domains, amounting to infringement of the Plaintiff’s copyright and broadcast reproduction rights; ii.Pass an order of injunction directing the Defendant Nos. 67 to 84, its directors, officers, affiliates, servants, employees, and all others in capacity of principal or agent acting for and, on their behalf, or anyone claiming through, by or under it, to suspend the domain name registration of the websites identified at Document ‘A’; This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. timeline.detail: Suit decreed.
timeline.orderText: CS(COMM) 756/2025 Page 1 of 4 $~58 * IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI + CS(COMM) 756/2025 & I.A. 19638/2025 JIOSTAR INDIA PRIVATE LIMITED .....Plaintiff Through: Mr. Siddharth Chopra, Mr. Yatinder Garg, Ms. Disha Sharma, Mr. Priyansh Kohli and Ms. Ishi Singh, Advocates versus HTTPS//IPTVWORLD.IN & ORS. .....Defendants Through: Mr. Shashi Kant Jha, Advocate with Ms. Komal Thakur, Advocate CORAM: HON'BLE MS. JUSTICE MANMEET PRITAM SINGH ARORA O R D E R % 12.08.2025 I.A. 19638/2025 (joint application under Order XXIII Rule 3 r/w Section 151 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 filed by the plaintiff and defendant no. 1) 1. The present application has been filed by the Plaintiff and Defendant no. 1 under Order XXIII Rule 3 of Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (‘CPC’) for recording the terms of settlement arrived between the parties vide Settlement Agreement dated 08.08.2025 and seeking a decree based on the said settlement. 2. The Settlement Agreement is annexed as document ‘A’ of the captioned application. 3. The Plaintiff’s authorized representative and Defendant no. 1 are personally present in Court. 4. Learned counsel for the Plaintiff states that Plaintiff is seeking a decree in terms of the prayer clause mentioned at paragraph 78(i) of the suit against Defendant no. 1. This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:58:19 CS(COMM) 756/2025 Page 2 of 4 4.1 He states that in view of the terms and conditions set out in the Settlement Agreement, the Plaintiff is not pressing its relief for damages and other consequential reliefs against Defendant no. 1. 4.2 He prays that the suit may be decreed qua Defendant No. 1 in terms of prayer clause paragraph 78(i) of the plaint and in terms of Settlement Agreement dated 08.08.2025. 5. Mr. Shashi Kant Jha, Advocate on behalf of Defendant no. 1 submits that the agreement has been signed by Defendant no. 1 after due negotiations and understanding of the terms and conditions set out therein. 6. Defendant no. 1, who is present in Court submits that he attended college for the course of B.Com, though he could not graduate. He states that he understands English language and he has signed this agreement after understanding the terms and conditions set out therein. 7. This Court has heard the learned counsel for the Plaintiff and the Defendant no. 1 and has perused the terms and conditions of the agreement. 8. The condition at clause ‘7’ of the Settlement Agreement, stating that Plaintiff shall be entitled to revive its claims (monetary claims) against the Defendant No. 1, is not tenable as the suit is being finally disposed of qua Defendant No. 1. The original clause 7 reads as under: - “7. That in the event of any breach of the terms of this Settlement Agreement by the Defendant No. I, including but not limited to breach of the injunction, failure to cooperate in the investigation, or any false or misleading disclosure, the Plaintiff shall be entitled to take appropriate legal action including revival of claims against the Defendant, pressing of criminal charges including those arising from FIR No. 0279, and pursuing damages as originally claimed in the suit. All rights and remedies of the Plaintiff in such event shall stand expressly reserved. “ This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:58:19 CS(COMM) 756/2025 Page 3 of 4 9. Learned counsel for the Plaintiff states, on instructions from the Plaintiff’s representative, that Plaintiff withdraws its right to initiate revival of monetary claims against the Defendant no. 1 in case of breach of the Settlement Agreement; however, the Plaintiff reserves its right to press criminal charges including those arising from FIR No. 0279. This statement of the Plaintiff is taken on record and it is bound down to the same. 10. The reference to the Settlement Agreement in this order will be read with the modification of clause 7 to the extent referred to in paragraph nos. 8 and 9 of this order. The modified clause 7 shall read as under: - 7. That in the event of any breach of the terms of this Settlement Agreement by the Defendant No. I, including but not limited to breach of the injunction, failure to cooperate in the investigation, or any false or misleading disclosure, the Plaintiff shall be entitled to take appropriate legal action including revival of claims against the Defendant, pressing of criminal charges including those arising from FIR No. 0279. All rights and remedies of the Plaintiff in such event shall stand expressly reserved. 8. This Court has perused the Settlement Agreement entered between the Plaintiff and Defendant no. 1, annexed as document ‘A’ in the captioned application, and is satisfied that the said settlement (as modified above) satisfies the requirements of Order XXIII Rule 3 CPC. The settlement is lawful and therefore, there is no impediment in decreeing the suit in terms of the settlement entered between the parties. 9. The statements and undertakings given by the parties are accepted by this Court and the parties are held bound by the same. 10. Consequently, the application is allowed, and the captioned suit is decreed qua Defendant no. 1 in terms of prayer clause 78(i) of the suit as well as the (modified) Settlement Agreement. This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:58:19 CS(COMM) 756/2025 Page 4 of 4 11. The Registry of this Court is directed to prepare a decree in terms of this order. The terms and conditions of the Settlement Agreement shall form part of the decree,

timeline.orderText: CS(COMM) 756/2025 Page 1 of 7 $~47 * IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI + CS(COMM) 756/2025 & I.A. 17944-17947/2025 JIOSTAR INDIA PRIVATE LIMITED .....Plaintiff Through: Mr. Siddharth Chopra, Mr. Yatinder Garg, Ms. Disha Sharma, Ms. Ishi Singh, Ms. Priyansh Kohli, Advocates versus HTTPS//IPTVWORLD.IN & ORS. .....Defendants Through: Mr. Shashikant Jha, Advocate for D-1 CORAM: HON'BLE MS. JUSTICE MANMEET PRITAM SINGH ARORA O R D E R % 29.07.2025 I.A. 17947/2025 1. This is an application seeking leave to file additional documents under Order XI Rule 1(4) of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (‘CPC’) [as amended by the Commercial Courts, Commercial Division and Commercial Appellate Division of High Courts Act, 2015 (‘Commercial Courts Act’)] read with Section 151 CPC. 2. The Plaintiff, if it wishes to file additional documents will file the same within thirty (30) days from today, and it shall do so strictly as per the provisions of the Commercial Courts Act and the Delhi High Court (Original Side) Rules, 2018 (‘DHC Rules’). 3. For the reasons stated in the application, the same is allowed. 4. Accordingly, the application is disposed of. I.A. 17945/2025 This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:58:19 CS(COMM) 756/2025 Page 2 of 7 5. This is an application under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 read with Section 151 CPC, filed by the Plaintiff seeking exemption from instituting pre-litigation mediation. 6. Having regard to the facts that the present suit contemplates urgent interim relief and in light of the judgement of the Supreme Court in Yamini Manohar v. T.K.D. Krithi 1 , exemption from the requirement of pre- institution mediation is granted to the plaintiff. 7. Accordingly, the application stands disposed of. I.A. 17946/2025 8. This application is filed by the Plaintiff under Section 80 read with Section 151 CPC seeking exemption from issuing notices to Defendant No. 17 (Department of Telecommunications [DoT]), Defendant No. 18 (Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology [MEITY]), Defendant No. 9 (BSNL), and Defendant No. 12 (MTNL) who are only added for compliance and no formal relief/remedy has been sought for against them. 9. In view of the averments made in the application, at paragraph 3, the application is allowed. I.A. 17944/2025 (Under Order XXXIX Rule 1 & 2 CPC) 10. The present application is filed by the Plaintiff under Order XXXIX Rule 1 and 2 CPC seeking ex-parte ad-interim injunction against Defendants for infringing copyright and broadcast reproduction rights of the Plaintiff by unlawfully providing access to the Plaintiff's exclusive content, including live channels and films, without authorization. 1 (2024) 5 SCC 815 This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:58:19 CS(COMM) 756/2025 Page 3 of 7 11. It is stated in the application that the Defendants operate an IPTV service through the impugned website and application that facilitate access to pirated content. The Plaintiff, which holds exclusive distribution rights for various channels and digital platforms, alleges that the Defendants engage in rampant piracy, harming its rights and revenue. 12. Mr. Komal Thakur has entered appearance on behalf of Defendant no. 1, and he is represented through advocate Mr. Shashikant Jha, learned counsel for Defendant No. 1. Learned counsel for Defendant No. 1 states that he has already e-filed his vakalatnama. He is directed to provide a copy to the counsel for the Plaintiff. 13. Learned counsel for Defendant No. 1 submits that Defendant no. 1 after being served with an advance copy of the suit has already taken steps for taking down the impugned website https://iptvworld.in. 13.1 He states that Defendant no. 1 is willing to cooperate with the Plaintiff and provide him with all relevant information available with him and taking all appropriate steps for removing Rogue websites and Web App. 13.2 He states that Defendant No. 1 is aware of approximately 300 more URLs and he will share the information with the Plaintiff. 13.3 He states that Defendant no. 1 acknowledges the proprietary rights of the Plaintiff and an injunction in terms of clause (i) of this application may be passed against Defendant No. 1. 14. Learned counsel for the Plaintiff states that the server domain http://junoon.fun:8080 is also required to be taken down and he prays that Defendant no. 4, which is the domain name registrar of the said website, be directed to take down the said domain name. He refers to an investigation This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:58:19 CS(COMM) 756/2025 Page 4 of 7 report filed as Document No. 9 to contend that the content is streaming on this server domain. He states that IPTV login server details provided to the customer are http://junoon.fun:8080. 14.1. In response, learned counsel for the Defendant no. 1 states that https://iptvworld.in is a run through server domain of http://junoon.fun:8080. 14.2. He states that Defendant no. 1 has no objection to the server domain http://junoon.fun:8080 being taken down. He states however Defendant no. 1 has no control over this domain name. He states that, in fact, in view of the submission of Defendant No. 1 this website also has to be taken down. 15. The statement of Defendant No. 1 is taken on record. The averments made in the plaint and the documents filed show that the server domain http://junoon.fun:8080 is streaming the copyrighted content of the Plaintiff. In these facts, the injunction in

JUSTICE MANMEET PRITAM SINGH ARORA O R D E R % 08.08.2025 I.A. 19338/2025 (seeking exemption from pre-institution mediation) 1. This is an application under Section 12A(1) of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 [‘Act of 2015’] read with Section 151 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 [‘CPC’], filed by the Plaintiff seeking exemption from instituting pre-litigation mediation. 2. JUSTICE JYOTI SINGH O R D E R % 26.02.2026 I.A. 5415/2026 (u/S 151 CPC) 1. This application is filed on behalf of the Plaintiff seeking exemption from effecting advance service on the Defendant. 2. For the reasons stated in the application, the same is allowed exempting Plaintiff from effecting advance service on the Defendant. 3. Application stands disposed of. I.A. 5416/2026 (for pre-institution mediation) 4. This application is filed on behalf of the Plaintiff under Section 12-A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 seeking exemption from Pre-Institution Mediation. 5. Having regard to the facts of the present case wherein urgent relief is prayed for and in light of the judgment of Supreme Court in Yamini This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:58:45 CS(COMM) 195/2026 Page 2 of 23 Manohar v. T.K.D. Keerthi, (2024) 5 SCC 815, as also Division Bench of this Court in Chandra Kishore Chaurasia v. RA Perfumery Works Private Ltd., 2022 SCC OnLine Del 3529, exemption is granted to the Plaintiff from Pre-Institution Mediation. 6. Application is allowed and disposed of. I.A. 5414/2026 (u/O XI Rule 1 (4) of Commercial Courts Act, 2015 r/w Section 151 CPC) 7. This application is filed on behalf of the Plaintiff seeking to place on record additional documents. 8. Plaintiff, if it wishes to file additional documents at a later stage, shall do so strictly in accordance with provisions of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015. 9. Application is allowed and disposed of. I.A. 5417/2026 (u/S 151 CPC) 10. This application is filed on behalf of the Plaintiff seeking permission to file documents in a pen drive containing evidence capturing the infringing activities of the Defendant. 11. For the reasons stated in the application, the same is allowed permitting the Plaintiff to file documents in a pen drive containing evidence capturing the infringing activities of the Defendant. 12. Application stands disposed of. CS(COMM) 195/2026 13. Let plaint be registered as a suit. 14. Upon filing of process fee, issue summons to the Defendant through all permissible modes, returnable before the learned Joint Registrar on 24.03.2026. This is a digitally signed order. Summons shall state that the written statement shall be filed by the Defendant within 30 days from the receipt of summons along with affidavit of admission/denial of the documents filed by the Plaintiff. 16. It will be open to the Plaintiff to file replication within 30 days from the date of receipt of written statement along with affidavit of admission/denial of documents filed by the Defendant. 17. If any of the parties wish to seek inspection of any documents, the same be sought and given the timeline prescribed in Delhi High Court (Original Side) Rules, 2018. 18. Learned Joint Registrar will carry out admission/denial of documents and marking of exhibits. I.A. 5413/2026 (u/O XXXIX Rules 1 and 2 r/w Section 151 CPC) 19. This application is filed on behalf of the Plaintiff under Order XXXIX Rules 1 and 2 read with Section 151 of CPC for grant of ex parte ad interim injunction. 20. Issue notice to the Defendant through all permissible modes, returnable before Court on 30.04.2026. 21. Case set out by the Plaintiff in the plaint is that Plaintiff is a growing company in the segment of fast-moving consumer goods (‘FMCG’) and a group company of Fortune 500 company. Along with RIL Group, Plaintiff has built a strong portfolio ranging from Campa, Raskik and Sosyo in the beverage category to Glimmer, Puric, Get Real and Dozo in the home and personal care segment. Under the Independence brand, Plaintiff offers a wide range of daily essentials to Indian consumers at exceptional value. 22. It is stated that since 1960s, Plaintiff’s group has evolved into one of India’s leading business houses with a workforce of over 1,90,000 people, This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:58:45 CS(COMM) 195/2026 Page 4 of 23 with business operations in various industries, including oil and gas exploration and production, petroleum refining and marketing, petrochemicals, yarn, fabrics and garments, telecommunication services, retail services, amongst others. RIL Group is the largest Indian private sector conglomerate which consists of 388 companies, of which 348 companies are subsidiaries of Reliance Industries Limited (RIL). The business of RIL and its group companies is spread within India and throughout the world including USA, Germany, Australia, UK, Netherlands, Malaysia, Singapore, Kenya, Uganda, U.A.E., Brazil, Mauritius, Rwanda, Zanzibar, Tanzania, etc. and substantial number of such companies are incorporated with their primary name as ‘Reliance’. 23. It is stated that vide Deed of Assignment dated 30.08.2022, Plaintiff group company acquired the age-old iconic beverage brand ‘CAMPA’ from its predecessors i.e., Campa Beverages Private Limited. CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE TUSHAR RAO GEDELA O R D E R % 13.04.2026 1. Learned counsel for the plaintiff submits that the subject trademark has been assigned by the plaintiff to another entity and necessitates filing of an application to ensure the new entity is brought on record in place of the plaintiff. Learned counsel seeks and is granted four weeks to file for the same. 2. Learned counsel submits that the parties are willing to explore the possibility of settlement of the inter-se disputes and seeks referral of the matter to mediation. 3. Accordingly, the parties are referred to mediation. 4. Parties are directed to appear before the Delhi High Court Mediation & Conciliation Centre on 18.04.2026 at 4.30 p.m. 5. The Organising Secretary of the Delhi High Court Mediation & Conciliation Centre is requested to nominate a Mediator well versed in the subject matter of the suit. 6. List before the Court on 21.08.2026, awaiting the outcome of the mediation proceedings. TUSHAR RAO GEDELA, J APRIL 13, 2026 kct This is a digitally signed order. Service-affidavit has been filed by the plaintiff, vide diary no. 6309918/2025 and as per same, the service via speed posts to the defendant was returned unserved with the report of “addressee cannot be located” and “the consignee shifted from the given address”. timeline.detail: I.A. 14258/2025 (Exemption from pre-litigation mediation) 1. The plaintiff, vide the present application under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, read with Section 151…
timeline.orderText: CS(COMM) 582/2025 Page 1 of 13 $~15 * IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI + CS(COMM) 582/2025 RELIANCE RETAIL LIMITED .....Plaintiff Through: Mr. Saikrishna Rajagopal, Mr. Yatinder Garg, Ms. Disha Sharma, Mr. Akshay Maloo and Ms. Ishi Singh, Advocates versus BALAJI FOODS AND BEVERAGE .....Defendant Through: None. CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SAURABH BANERJEE O R D E R % 30.05.2025 I.A. 14258/2025 (Exemption from pre-litigation mediation) 1. The plaintiff, vide the present application under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, read with Section 151 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (CPC), seeks exemption from instituting pre-litigation mediation. 2. Considering the averments made in the present application, as also since the plaintiff is seeking grant of an ex-parte ad interim injunction in an accompanying application as also appointment of a Local Commissioner, and in view of the judgment passed by the Hon’ble Supreme Court in Yamini Manohar v. T.K.D. Krithi 2024 (5) SCC 815, which has been followed by a Division Bench of this Court in Chandra Kishore Chaurasia v. R. A. Perfumery Works Private Limited 2022:DHC:4454-DB, the plaintiff is exempted from instituting pre- litigation mediation. This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:59:00 CS(COMM) 582/2025 Page 2 of 13 3. Accordingly, the present application is allowed and disposed of. I.A. 14257/2025 (Exemption from advance service) 4. The plaintiff, vide the present application under Section 151 of the CPC, seek exemption from effecting advance service upon the defendant. 5. Learned counsel for the plaintiff submits that considering the position involved, particularly since appointment of a Local Commissioner for execution of search and seizure of evidence/ counterfeit products at the premises of defendant is being sought in an accompanying application, there is a likelihood that the apprehensions of the plaintiff will become a reality in case advance service is effected and therefore, an exemption from effecting advance service is necessary. 6. For the reasons stated in the application as also taking into account the aforesaid factors and in the interest of justice, the plaintiff is granted exemption from effecting advance service upon the defendant. 7. Accordingly, the present application is allowed and disposed of. I.A. 14256/2025 (Additional documents) 8. The plaintiff, vide the present application under Order XI Rule 1(4) read with Section 151 of the CPC, seeks leave of this Court to file additional documents. 9. The plaintiff will be at liberty to file additional documents at a later stage, albeit, after initiating appropriate steps, strictly as per the provisions of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 read with Section 151 of the CPC and the Delhi High Court (Original Side) Rules, 2018. 10. Accordingly, the present application is allowed and disposed of. I.A. 14259/2025 (Leave to file documents in a pen-drive) 11. The plaintiff, vide the present application under Section 151 of the This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:59:00 CS(COMM) 582/2025 Page 3 of 13 CPC, seeks leave of this Court to file documents in a pen-drive containing the evidence and video clip capturing the infringing activities of the defendant alongwith supporting affidavit. 12. In terms of Rule 24 of Chapter XI of the Delhi High Court (Original Side) Rules 2018, let the electronic record by way of an encrypted CD/ DVD/ Pen-Drive Medium with a hash value in a non-edited form be filed before the Registry within a period of four weeks. 13. Let the same accordingly form a part of the record of the present suit by way of an electronic folder in such a manner that it can be opened to view by this Court as and when required. Also let the hash value be kept separately by the Registry on the file. 14. Accordingly, the present application is allowed and disposed of. CS(COMM) 582/2025 15. The plaintiff, by way of the present plaint, seeks grant of a permanent injunction for infringement of the trademark ‘CAMPA’ registered in its name, unfair trade practices, dilution and damages. 16. Let the plaint be registered as a suit. 17. Upon filing of the process fee, issue summons of the suit to the defendant through all permissible modes, returnable before the learned Joint Registrar on 04.09.2025. 18. Needless to say, it shall be specified in the summons that the written statement shall be filed by the defendant within a period of thirty days from the date of the receipt of the summons. Written statement shall be accompanied by affidavit of admission/ denial of documents of the plaintiff, without which the written statement shall not be taken on record. 19. Replication thereto, if any, be filed by the plaintiff within a period This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:59:00 CS(COMM) 582/2025 Page 4 of 13 of fifteen days from the date of receipt of written statement. The said replication, if any, shall also be accompanied by affidavit of admission/ denial of documents filed by the defendant, without which the replication shall not be taken on record within the aforesaid period of fifteen days. 20. If, and when, any of the parties wish to seek inspection of any document(s), the same shall be sought and given within the requisite timelines. 21. List before the learned Joint Registrar for marking exhibits of documents on 04.09.2025. It is made clear that if any party unjustifiably denies any document(s), then such party would be liable to be burdened w

Defendant nos. 1 to 9 are online copyright infringers. Defendant nos. 10 to 19 are DNRs. Defendant no. 20 is Whatsapp. Defendant no. 21 is Telegram. Defendant nos. 22 to 26 are specific entities who have been given directions by the Hon’ble Court to comply with as their presence was necessary for the said purpose. Defendant nos. 27 to 35 are various ISPs. Defendant nos. 36 and 37 are DoT & MEITY. 3. Notice was issued to defendant nos. 1 to 37 but the reply was to be only filed by defendant nos. 1 to 9. Notice on behalf of defendant no. 21 was accepted before the Hon’ble Court. As per the affidavit of service, all these defendants were served on 15.01.2026 through email. There is no appearance on behalf of defendant nos. 1 to 9. 4. Defendant nos. 10 to 37 were directed to file their respective compliance affidavits in regard to the specific compliances directed by the Hon’ble Court. 5. From amongst DNR’s i.e. defendant no. 10 to 19. Ld. Counsel for defendant no. 12 Porkbun LLC and defendant no. 19 Hosting Concepts BV submits that they have already complied with the directions regarding locking and suspending the domain names and have also supplied the registrant details to the counsel for the plaintiff. Compliance affidavit shall also be filed within 4 weeks, as per the submission. 6. Ld. Counsel for defendant no. 20 Whatsapp submits that they have already suspended the accounts of the numbers highlighted by the plaintiff and are in the process of supplying the BSI details to ld. Counsel for the plaintiff. 7. Defendant no. 21 has already complied with the orders of the Hon’ble Court and its compliance affidavit is already on record. This is a digitally signed order. Ld. counsel further submits that counsel for the plaintiff has been informed of the correct PayPal entity with whom these accounts are registered. Consequently, on the oral request of ld. Counsel for defendant no. 22 to which ld. Counsel for the plaintiff has no objection, defendant no. 22 stands deleted. 9. Defendant no. 23 Shopify Commerce has already complied with the orders of the Hon’ble Court and its compliance affidavit is on record. 10. There is no appearance on behalf of defendant nos. 24 to 26 and ld. Counsel for the plaintiff has not received any compliance intimations from these defendants. 11. Defendant nos. 27 to 35 are various ISPs who were given directions to block access to the infringing websites of defendant nos. 1 to 9. There is no appearance on behalf of the ISPs nor any compliance intimations have been received by ld. Counsel for the plaintiff. 12. Ld. Counsel for the plaintiff. Compliance affidavit be filed within four weeks. 13. Compliance affidavit has also been filed by the plaintiff under Order 39 Rule 3 CPC. 14. timeline.orderText: CS(COMM) 269/2024Page 1 of 3 $~18 *IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI +CS(COMM) 269/2024 and I.A. 7290/2024, I.A. 7292/2024 & RAZORPAY SOFTWARE PRIVATE LIMITED & ANR......Plaintiffs Through:Ms. Astha Sehgal, Advocate. versus JOHN DOE & ORS......Defendants Through:Mr. Vivek Reddy, Senior Advocate with Ms. Swati Agarwal, Mr. Mohit Singh, Mr. Vaarish Sawlani & Ms. Rithika Mathur, Advocates for D-6. Mr. Varun Pathak, Mr. Amee Rana & Ms. Sana Banyal, Advocates for D-7. Mr. Madhav Khosla, Advocate for D- 8. Ms. Nidhi Raman, CGSC with Mr. Debasish Mishra, Mr. Gokul Sharma, GP for D-9 & 10. Mr. Abhishek Anand, Advocate for D-11. Mr. K.G. Gopalakrishnan, Ms. Nisha Mohandas & Mr. Kunwar Raj Singh, Advocates. CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AMIT BANSAL O R D E R %28.04.2025 I.A. 38259/2024 (under Order XXXIX Rule 4 of CPC) 1.This application has been filed on behalf of the defendant no. 6/ WhatsApp LLC, seeking vacation/ modification of paragraph no. 20.4 of the ex-parteinterim order passed by this Court on 2 nd April, 2024. This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:59:54 CS(COMM) 269/2024Page 2 of 3 2.The relevant extract of the order of which the modification is sought, is set out below: “20.4. Defendant No. 6, WhatsApp LLC, is directed to file within four weeks from today, the Basic Subscriber Information relating to the WhatsApp accounts of the phone numbers +91-6398023058 and +91- 8103258963, as is available with them, in a sealed cover. A copy of the said documents shall also be provided to counsel for Plaintiffs, which shall be strictly used by them for the purpose of investigation and identification of the perpetrators.” 3.It is stated that insofar as the numbers which are the subject matter of the directions passed in paragraph no. 20.4 are concerned, the plaintiffs have not provided requisite evidence in support of its allegations. 4.Counsel for the plaintiffs has taken me to the evidence placed on record. 5.Insofar as the number +91 8103258963 is concerned, attention of the Court has been drawn to the complaint filed by the alleged victim (page no. 200 of the plaintiffs’ documents filed with the plaint). 6.In the complaint, the victim has clearly stated that the owner of the aforesaid number told the victim that he/ she worked in the plaintiff company, and called upon the victim to part with money. 7.Insofar as the number +91-6398023058 is concerned, attention of this Court has been drawn to page nos. 171 and 166 of the plaintiffs’ documents filed with the plaint. 8.In the screenshots of the WhatsApp messages filed therein, at page no. 171 of the plaintiffs’ documents, a reference has been given to a Telegram channelhttps://t.me/freestyle4425, which as per the plaintiff has been used to defraud victims. This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:59:54 CS(COMM) 269/2024Page 3 of 3 9.A reference to page no. 166 would show that the owner of the said number had introduced himself as the manager of the plaintiff company, and has included the complainant in the aforesaid Telegram group. 10.In my considered view, the aforesaid evidence is sufficient at this stage, at least for the purposes of disclosure of the Basic Subscriber Information (‘BSI’) of the owners of the said numbers. 10.1.The BSI details shall be filed by the applicant/defendant no.6 in a sealed cover, before this Court, within two (2) weeks from today. 10.2.A copy of the BSI information would also be provided to the counsel for the plaintiffs. 11.It is made clear that the plaintiffs shall use the said information only for the purposes of the present suit. 12.In view of the order passed above, the application is accordingly disposed of. CS(COMM) 269/2024 13.Since the defendants no. 3, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 15 and 18 have complied with the order dated 2 nd April, 2024, counsel for the plaintiffs submits that she has no difficulty, if the aforesaid defendants are deleted from the array of parties. 13.1.It is ordered accordingly. 14.Amended memo of parties be filed within one (1) week. 15.List on 10 th September, 2025. AMIT BANSAL, J APRIL 28, 2025/at This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:59:54

The Plaintiffs allege that Defendant No. 1 is perpetrating fraud on the general public by creating a false association with them, resulting in grave financial losses to the public. Considering the nature of controversy involved in the present suit as well as the peculiar facts and circumstances of the case, exemption from effecting advance service on Defendant No. 1 is allowed. 2. The Plaintiffs are renowned payment gateway service providers, specializing in developing application programme interface for a variety of financial products and other digital financial services, such as payment and payroll processing. The “RAZORPAY” product profile entails a portfolio of well- recognized technological products, such as, payment gateway, links and pages and payroll. 17.3. Particulars of registration have been set put in paragraph No. 23 of the plaint. 17.4. Plaintiffs jointly operate the domain name “www.razorpay.com”, which has been registered in the name of Plaintiff No. 2. JUSTICE TEJAS KARIA O R D E R %01.12.2025 I.A. 29944/2025 1.This is an Application filed under Order XXXIX Rule 2A of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, read with Section 12 of the Contempt of Courts Act, 1971, for taking action against the Defendant / Contemnor for wilful disobedience and violation of the Order dated 19.09.2025. 2.The learned Senior Counsel for the Plaintiffs submits that during the course of the execution of the Local Commission on 29.09.2025, the Defendant informed the learned Local Commissioner that the Defendant has stopped using the products with the Mark ‘ROZANA’ (“Impugned Mark”) almost eight-nine months prior to 29.09.2025. The Defendant has also submitted the statement before the learned Local Commissioner showing the last use of the Impugned Mark around February-March 2025. 3.The learned Senior Counsel for the Plaintiff submits that the said statement was apparently incorrect as the Defendant is still violating the This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 14:00:07 Order dated 19.09.2025 by commercially exporting the products with the Impugned Mark as late as June 2025, which is evident from the document annexed along with this Application and which shows that the products with the Impugned Mark are available at Lulu Hyper Market in Qatar, where the distributor / agent / franchise / partner / associate / dealer of the Defendant are inviting the customers to avail the special sale. 4.The learned Senior Counsel for the Plaintiff submits that in terms of the injunction dated 19.09.2025 even export of products bearing the Impugned Mark shall amount to violation of the said order under Section 56 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999. 5.Issue Notice. Let the Notice be served upon the Defendant / Contemnor through all permissible modes. 6.Let the Reply to this Application be filed within a period of four weeks from date. Rejoinder thereto, if any, be filed before the next date of hearing. 7.It is directed that the Defendant / Contemnor shall strictly comply with the interim injunction grantedvideOrder dated 19.09.2025 and stop using, manufacturing, selling and exporting any goods or products bearing the Impugned Mark ‘ROZANA’. 8.List on 14.01.2026, the date already fixed. JUSTICE SAURABH BANERJEE O R D E R % 08.07.2025 I.A. 15641/2025-By plaintiffs U/O 1 R 10 seeking impleadment of GODADDY.COM LLC as domain name Registrant of the additional identified rogue domains engaged in infringing activities and extension of ad-interim order dated 28.05.2025 1. Since by virtue of the present application, the plaintiffs seek impleadment of DNR Godaddy.com LLC as the defendant no.24, learned counsel appearing for the proposed defendant no.24, at the outset, submits though there are reservations/ objections about the averments made in the present suit, however, in all fairness submits that since the defendant no.24 may be a concerned party. 2. In view of the aforesaid, learned counsel for the plaintiffs, without This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 14:00:23 going into the merits of the present application, seeks leave to withdraw the present application and orally requests this Court for impleading the said proposed defendant i.e. DNR Godaddy.com LLC as defendant no.24 in the present suit as it has not been impleaded due to sheer inadvertence. 3. Considering the nature of the proceedings, and the nature of disputes involved it is deemed necessary for impleading the proposed defendant no.24 as it is a proper party herein. Furthermore, since today numerous websites, which may be as many as more than fifty (50), are still (wrongly) operating despite the specific order dated 28.05.2025 passed by this Court in which the DNR is Godaddy.com LLC, this Court is of the view that the proposed defendant no.24 is a necessary and proper party in the present suit, which due to share inadvertence, was not impleaded as a party at the initial stage. In any event, the present proceedings are at the initial stages when the impleadment of the proposed defendant herein will not cause any impediment in the way(s) of any of the defendants herein. 4. In view of the aforesaid, DNR Godaddy.com LLC is impleaded as defendant no.24 in the present proceedings. As such, the amended memo of parties filed along with the present application is taken on record. 5. Accordingly, the order passed on 28.05.2025 shall stand extended to the defendant no.24. 6. In view of the aforesaid directions, the present application stands disposed of. CS(COMM) 536/2025 & I.A. 13756/2025-Stay 7. Accepted. It has been submitted by the ld. counsel for the plaintiff that the FIFA World Cup is till 14.07.2025 and any delay in passing order on the application will further prejudice the right of the plaintiff and the order dated 28.05.2025 may be extended to the proposed defendant GODADDY.COM, on the other hand, ld. counsel for the defendant GODADDY.COM This is a digitally signed order. Vide the present application under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, read with Section 151 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (CPC), the plaintiff seeks exemption from pre-litigation mediation. 2. Considering the averments made in the present application, as also since the plaintiffs are seeking ex parte ad interim injunction in an accompanying application, and in view of the judgment passed by the Hon’ble Supreme Court in Yamini Manohar v. Accordingly, the present application is allowed and disposed of. CS(COMM) 535/2025 4.
